Chapter 6: Data Visualization with ggplot2

R for Management Consultants | BK Advisors

BK Advisors

2026-02-28

Learning Objectives

After this chapter, you will be able to:

  • Explain the grammar of graphics — data, aesthetics, and geometries
  • Build bar charts with geom_col() and geom_bar()
  • Create scatter plots with geom_point() and add trend lines
  • Plot time series with geom_line() for monthly and quarterly trends
  • Visualize distributions with geom_histogram() and geom_boxplot()
  • Choose the right chart type for common consulting questions

Chapter Overview

Grammar of
Graphics
→
Bar
Charts
→
Scatter
Plots
→
Line
Charts
→
Histograms
& Box Plots

The Grammar of Graphics

A layered system for building any chart

What Is the Grammar of Graphics?

The grammar of graphics is a framework that decomposes every chart into independent components: data, aesthetic mappings, and geometric objects. Instead of memorizing chart types, you combine these building blocks to create any visualization.

The three essential layers:

Layer What It Does Example
Data The dataset you want to visualize facility_data
Aesthetics (aes) Maps variables to visual properties x-axis, y-axis, color, size
Geometry (geom_*) The visual shape drawn on the plot Points, bars, lines

This is why ggplot2 is so powerful for consulting work: once you learn the grammar, you can build any chart — not just the ones someone pre-built for you.

The Three-Layer Pattern

library(ggplot2)

# Layer 1: DATA — what are we plotting?
ggplot(data = facility_data,

# Layer 2: AESTHETICS — which variables map to which visual properties?
       aes(x = country, y = nmr)) +

# Layer 3: GEOMETRY — what shape represents the data?
  geom_col()

Every ggplot follows this pattern: ggplot(data, aes(...)) + geom_*(). The + operator adds layers — you can stack as many geometries, labels, and themes as you need.

Setting Up the Example Data

library(tidyverse)

# Country-level NMR summary (pre-calculated)
country_summary <- tibble(
  country = c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi", "Mozambique", "Kenya"),
  avg_nmr = c(29.1, 25.3, 22.7, 31.8, 20.4),
  total_deliveries = c(12500, 9800, 7200, 5400, 11300)
)

# Facility-level data
facility_data <- tibble(
  facility_id = paste0("F", sprintf("%03d", 1:20)),
  country = rep(c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi", "Mozambique", "Kenya"), each = 4),
  facility_type = rep(c("Hospital", "Health Center", "Health Center", "Health Post"), 5),
  delivery_volume = c(320, 145, 160, 45, 280, 130, 155, 38,
                      210, 95, 110, 32, 175, 80, 90, 28,
                      290, 125, 140, 42),
  nmr = c(25.3, 31.7, 28.9, 42.1, 22.8, 27.5, 24.3, 38.6,
          19.8, 25.1, 22.4, 35.3, 28.7, 33.9, 30.1, 45.2,
          18.5, 23.2, 20.7, 32.8)
)

# Monthly trend data
monthly_trends <- tibble(
  month = rep(seq(as.Date("2024-01-01"), as.Date("2024-12-01"), by = "month"), 3),
  country = rep(c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi"), each = 12),
  nmr = c(
    29.1, 28.5, 27.8, 27.2, 26.5, 26.0, 25.3, 24.8, 24.1, 23.5, 23.0, 22.4,
    25.3, 25.0, 24.6, 24.1, 23.7, 23.2, 22.8, 22.3, 21.9, 21.4, 21.0, 20.5,
    22.7, 22.3, 21.8, 21.4, 20.9, 20.5, 20.0, 19.6, 19.1, 18.7, 18.2, 17.8
  )
)

Aesthetics — Mapping Variables to Visuals

# x and y position
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = delivery_volume, y = nmr)) +
  geom_point()

# Add color to distinguish countries
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = delivery_volume, y = nmr, color = country)) +
  geom_point()

# Add size to encode a third variable
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = delivery_volume, y = nmr,
                           color = country, size = delivery_volume)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7)

Aesthetics inside aes() are mapped to variables — ggplot2 picks colors and sizes automatically. Aesthetics outside aes() are fixed for all points (e.g., geom_point(color = "red", size = 3)).

Bar Charts

Comparing categories with geom_col() and geom_bar()

geom_col() — Pre-Calculated Values

# When your data already has the values to plot (e.g., summary table)
ggplot(country_summary, aes(x = country, y = avg_nmr)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#005CB9") +
  labs(
    title = "Average Neonatal Mortality Rate by Country",
    x = NULL,
    y = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)"
  )

geom_col() plots bars where the height equals the value in your data. Use it when you already have the numbers — summary tables, pre-calculated means, or aggregated indicators.

geom_bar() — Counting Rows

# When you want ggplot to COUNT the rows for you
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = facility_type)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "#3E9B6E") +
  labs(
    title = "Number of Facilities by Type",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Count"
  )

geom_bar() counts rows automatically — you only provide x. Use it for frequency tables: how many facilities per type, how many countries per region, how many months with stockouts.

Stacked Bars — Two Variables

# Stacked bar: facility type counts by country
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = country, fill = facility_type)) +
  geom_bar() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Hospital"      = "#005CB9",
    "Health Center"  = "#3E9B6E",
    "Health Post"    = "#FA7650"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Facility Type Distribution by Country",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of Facilities",
    fill = "Facility Type"
  )

Stacked bars show composition — what share of facilities in each country are hospitals vs. health centers vs. health posts. Add position = "fill" inside geom_bar() to convert counts to proportions.

Horizontal Bars with reorder() and coord_flip()

# Horizontal bars, ordered by value — the consulting standard
ggplot(country_summary, aes(x = reorder(country, avg_nmr), y = avg_nmr)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#005CB9") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "NMR by Country (Ranked)",
    x = NULL,
    y = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)"
  )

reorder(country, avg_nmr) sorts the bars by value instead of alphabetically. coord_flip() turns them horizontal — easier to read when you have long category labels. This is the standard chart format in most consulting slide decks.

Bar Chart Variations — Summary

You have… Use Key argument
Pre-calculated values (mean NMR) geom_col() fill = "#005CB9"
Raw data to count geom_bar() (no y needed)
Two categories geom_bar(aes(fill = ...)) Stacked by default
Side-by-side groups geom_bar(position = "dodge") Groups next to each other
Proportions geom_bar(position = "fill") Bars sum to 100%
Long labels Add coord_flip() Horizontal bars

Scatter Plots

Exploring relationships between two continuous variables

geom_point() — Basic Scatter Plot

# Relationship between delivery volume and NMR
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = delivery_volume, y = nmr)) +
  geom_point(color = "#005CB9", size = 3, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "Delivery Volume vs. Neonatal Mortality Rate",
    x = "Monthly Delivery Volume",
    y = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)"
  )

Scatter plots answer: “Is there a relationship between these two numbers?” Here we are asking whether higher-volume facilities tend to have lower neonatal mortality rates — a question that comes up frequently in MNH program evaluations.

Color by Country

# Add country as a color dimension
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = delivery_volume, y = nmr, color = country)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Ethiopia"   = "#005CB9",
    "Tanzania"   = "#3E9B6E",
    "Malawi"     = "#00A1DF",
    "Mozambique" = "#FA7650",
    "Kenya"      = "#E24A3F"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Delivery Volume vs. NMR by Country",
    x = "Monthly Delivery Volume",
    y = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)",
    color = "Country"
  )

Mapping color = country inside aes() lets you see whether the volume-NMR relationship holds across all countries or differs by context. Each country gets its own color automatically — or you can assign specific brand colors with scale_color_manual().

Adding a Trend Line with geom_smooth()

# Add a linear trend line
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = delivery_volume, y = nmr)) +
  geom_point(color = "#005CB9", size = 3, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "#E24A3F", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Delivery Volume vs. NMR with Trend Line",
    subtitle = "Linear regression fit with 95% confidence interval",
    x = "Monthly Delivery Volume",
    y = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)"
  )

geom_smooth(method = "lm") adds a linear regression line. The gray shaded band is the 95% confidence interval. Set se = FALSE to hide it. You can also use method = "loess" for a curved fit when the relationship is non-linear.

Trend Lines by Group

# Separate trend line for each country
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = delivery_volume, y = nmr, color = country)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Ethiopia"   = "#005CB9",
    "Tanzania"   = "#3E9B6E",
    "Malawi"     = "#00A1DF",
    "Mozambique" = "#FA7650",
    "Kenya"      = "#E24A3F"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Volume-NMR Relationship by Country",
    subtitle = "Each line is a separate linear fit per country",
    x = "Monthly Delivery Volume",
    y = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)",
    color = "Country"
  )

When color = country is in the global aes(), both geom_point() and geom_smooth() inherit it. Each country gets its own trend line — making it easy to see whether the relationship is consistent or diverges across contexts.

Line Charts

Tracking trends over time with geom_line()

geom_line() — Single Time Series

# Filter to one country
ethiopia_trends <- monthly_trends |>
  filter(country == "Ethiopia")

# NMR trend for Ethiopia
ggplot(ethiopia_trends, aes(x = month, y = nmr)) +
  geom_line(color = "#005CB9", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(color = "#005CB9", size = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "Neonatal Mortality Rate Trend --- Ethiopia",
    subtitle = "Monthly NMR, January--December 2024",
    x = NULL,
    y = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)"
  )

Line charts answer: “How is this indicator changing over time?” Adding geom_point() on top of geom_line() marks each data point, making it clear where the actual observations are.

Multiple Countries on One Chart

# NMR trends for all three countries
ggplot(monthly_trends, aes(x = month, y = nmr, color = country)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Ethiopia" = "#005CB9",
    "Tanzania" = "#3E9B6E",
    "Malawi"   = "#00A1DF"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "NMR Trends by Country",
    subtitle = "Monthly neonatal mortality rate, 2024",
    x = NULL,
    y = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)",
    color = NULL
  )

The group aesthetic tells ggplot2 which points to connect. When you use color = country, grouping is handled automatically. Use scale_color_manual() to assign BKA brand colors so each country is instantly recognizable.

Line Chart with Annotations

# Add reference line and annotation
ggplot(monthly_trends, aes(x = month, y = nmr, color = country)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 12, linetype = "dashed", color = "#999999") +
  annotate("text", x = as.Date("2024-10-01"), y = 13,
           label = "SDG Target: 12", color = "#999999", size = 3.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Ethiopia" = "#005CB9",
    "Tanzania" = "#3E9B6E",
    "Malawi"   = "#00A1DF"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "NMR Trends vs. SDG Target",
    subtitle = "All countries still above the SDG 3.2 target of 12 per 1,000",
    x = NULL,
    y = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)",
    color = NULL
  )

geom_hline() adds a horizontal reference line — ideal for targets, thresholds, or benchmarks. annotate() places a text label at a specific position. These two layers turn a simple trend chart into a story about progress toward a goal.

Histograms & Box Plots

Understanding distributions

geom_histogram() — Distribution of a Single Variable

# Distribution of NMR across all facilities
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = nmr)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = "#005CB9", color = "white") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Neonatal Mortality Rates",
    subtitle = "Across all reporting facilities (n = 20)",
    x = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)",
    y = "Number of Facilities"
  )

Histograms answer: “How are the values spread out?” The binwidth argument controls the width of each bar. Too narrow and you see noise; too wide and you lose the shape. Start with a reasonable value and adjust.

Overlapping Histograms — Comparing Groups

# Compare NMR distributions by facility type
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = nmr, fill = facility_type)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, alpha = 0.6, position = "identity", color = "white") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Hospital"      = "#005CB9",
    "Health Center"  = "#3E9B6E",
    "Health Post"    = "#FA7650"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "NMR Distribution by Facility Type",
    x = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)",
    y = "Number of Facilities",
    fill = "Facility Type"
  )

Use position = "identity" with alpha (transparency) to overlay histograms. This reveals whether hospitals have a tighter NMR distribution than health posts — a common finding in MNH program data.

geom_boxplot() — Comparing Groups

# NMR distribution by country
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = country, y = nmr, fill = country)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Ethiopia"   = "#005CB9",
    "Tanzania"   = "#3E9B6E",
    "Malawi"     = "#00A1DF",
    "Mozambique" = "#FA7650",
    "Kenya"      = "#E24A3F"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "NMR Distribution by Country",
    x = NULL,
    y = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)"
  )

A box plot shows five summary statistics: minimum, first quartile (Q1), median, third quartile (Q3), and maximum. The box spans Q1 to Q3 (the middle 50% of values). Outliers appear as individual points beyond the whiskers.

Box Plot by Facility Type

# NMR by facility type within each country
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = country, y = nmr, fill = facility_type)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Hospital"      = "#005CB9",
    "Health Center"  = "#3E9B6E",
    "Health Post"    = "#FA7650"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "NMR by Country and Facility Type",
    subtitle = "Health posts consistently show higher NMR across countries",
    x = NULL,
    y = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)",
    fill = "Facility Type"
  )

Grouped box plots let you compare distributions across two dimensions at once: country and facility type. This reveals patterns like health posts having both higher and more variable NMR than hospitals — a finding that drives resource allocation decisions.

Choosing the Right Chart

Question You Are Asking Chart Type Geom
How does indicator X compare across categories? Bar chart geom_col()
How many facilities are in each group? Bar chart (count) geom_bar()
Is there a relationship between X and Y? Scatter plot geom_point()
How is this indicator changing over time? Line chart geom_line()
How are values distributed? Histogram geom_histogram()
How do distributions compare across groups? Box plot geom_boxplot()

Start with the question, not the chart type. The right visualization follows directly from what you are trying to show your stakeholders. If you are comparing categories, use bars. If you are showing a trend, use lines. If you are exploring a relationship, use points.

Key Takeaways

Every ggplot follows the same pattern: ggplot(data, aes(...)) + geom_*()

geom_col() for pre-calculated values, geom_bar() for counting rows, geom_point() for relationships, geom_line() for trends, geom_histogram() and geom_boxplot() for distributions.

Start with the question you are answering — the chart type follows from the question.

Looking Ahead

  • Labels, scales, and axis formatting
  • Faceting with facet_wrap() for small multiples
  • Multi-panel layouts with patchwork
  • Exporting charts at presentation resolution with ggsave()
